cards.question: In ancient times man used to move only on foot and carry goods either on his back or on the back of some animals.
cards.text_html: In ancient times man used to move only on foot and carry goods either on his back or on the back of some animals.

cards.question: A great change in the modes of transport was made: by the invention of the wheel. by the invention of the steams engine.
cards.text_html:
A great change in the modes of transport was made:
  • by the invention of the wheel.
  • by the invention of the steams engine.

cards.question: The invention of the wheel made a great change in the modes of transport. The wheels were used in bullock carts, horse carts, and camel carts, etc. Such vehicles which moved on wheels were pulled by animals.
cards.text_html: The invention of the wheel made a great change in the modes of transport. The wheels were used in bullock carts, horse carts, and camel carts, etc. Such vehicles which moved on wheels were pulled by animals.

cards.question: The invention of the internal combustion engine which uses petrol or diesel as fuel in the second half of the 19th century gave us transport vehicles called automobiles such as cars, buses, trucks, etc.
cards.text_html: The invention of the internal combustion engine which uses petrol or diesel as fuel in the second half of the 19th century gave us transport vehicles called automobiles such as cars, buses, trucks, etc.

cards.question: Aeroplanes were discovered as a means of transport in the early 20 th century. Electric trains, monorail, supersonic aeroplanes and spacecrafts are also 20 th century contributions to the modes of transport.
cards.text_html: Aeroplanes were discovered as a means of transport in the early 20th century. Electric trains, monorail, supersonic aeroplanes and spacecrafts are also 20th century contributions to the modes of transport.

cards.question: Measurement means the comparison of an unknown quantity with some known quantity. This fixed quantity is called a unit .
cards.text_html: Measurement means the comparison of an unknown quantity with some known quantity. This fixed quantity is called a unit.

cards.question: Non-standard measures: The lengths of steps, arms, hands, or fingers of different people are different, therefore the distance measured with their help is not always reliable. These methods are, therefore, called non-standard measures.
cards.text_html: Non-standard measures: The lengths of steps, arms, hands, or fingers of different people are different, therefore the distance measured with their help is not always reliable. These methods are, therefore, called non-standard measures.

cards.question: Standard measures: Measures that are the same all over the world are known as standard measures.
cards.text_html: Standard measures: Measures that are the same all over the world are known as standard measures.

cards.question: The system of units now used is known as the International System of Units (SI Units) .
cards.text_html: The system of units now used is known as the International System of Units (SI Units).

cards.question: The SI unit of length is a metre. Kilometre is a larger unit of measuring length or distance.
cards.text_html: The SI unit of length is a metre. Kilometre is a larger unit of measuring length or distance.

cards.question: Simple multiples of units: Units that are used for the measurement of larger distances are the multiples of SI unit. For example: deca, hecto, kilo.
cards.text_html: Simple multiples of units: Units that are used for the measurement of larger distances are the multiples of SI unit. For example: deca, hecto, kilo.

cards.question: 1 deca metre = 10 m 1 hecto metre = 100 m 1 kilometre = 1000 m
cards.text_html:
1 deca metre = 10 m
1 hecto metre = 100 m
1 kilometre = 1000 m

cards.question: Sub-multiples of units: Units used for measuring smaller distances are the sub-multiples of SI units. For example, milli, centi, deci. 1 m = 10 decimetre 1 m = 100 centimetre 1 m = 1000 millimetre.
cards.text_html:
Sub-multiples of units: Units used for measuring smaller distances are the sub-multiples of SI units.
For example, milli, centi, deci.
1 m = 10 decimetre
1 m = 100 centimetre
1 m = 1000 millimetre.

cards.question: Length is the distance between two points.
cards.text_html: Length is the distance between two points.

cards.question: Metre i s used as a standard unit for measuring the length of an object. It is written in short form as ‘m’.
cards.text_html: Metre is used as a standard unit for measuring the length of an object. It is written in short form as ‘m’.

cards.question: 1 metre = 100 centimetres 1 centimetre = 10 millimetres
cards.text_html:
1 metre = 100 centimetres
1 centimetre = 10 millimetres

cards.question: The type of unit used depends on the magnitude of the length to be measured. For example - a meter can be used as a unit to measure the length of a table, the length of a room, the height of a tree, or a building. A pencil or a notebook has a small length than a meter so it is measured in centimeters.
cards.text_html: The type of unit used depends on the magnitude of the length to be measured. For example - a meter can be used as a unit to measure the length of a table, the length of a room, the height of a tree, or a building. A pencil or a notebook has a small length than a meter so it is measured in centimeters.

cards.question: The various type of measuring devices used for measuring length are metre scale and measuring tape.
cards.text_html: The various type of measuring devices used for measuring length are metre scale and measuring tape.

cards.question: Precautions while using a scale: The scale should be placed parallel to the side of the object being measured. While reading the scale, the eye must be placed vertically above the scale mark being read. If the scale has a damaged zero mark or broken left end, measure the length of an object starting from 1 cm mark of scale and then subtract 1 cm from the reading taken at the right end to get the actual length of the object.
cards.text_html:
Precautions while using a scale:

  1. The scale should be placed parallel to the side of the object being measured.
  2. While reading the scale, the eye must be placed vertically above the scale mark being read.
  3. If the scale has a damaged zero mark or broken left end, measure the length of an object starting from 1 cm mark of scale and then subtract 1 cm from the reading taken at the right end to get the actual length of the object.

cards.question: Least count: A scale is marked in centimeters and millimeters. With scales of this kind we can measure correctly up to one millimeter, that is one-tenth of a centimeter. This is called the least count of a (15 cm) scale.
cards.text_html: Least count: A scale is marked in centimeters and millimeters. With scales of this kind we can measure correctly up to one millimeter, that is one-tenth of a centimeter. This is called the least count of a (15 cm) scale.

topics.topic_name: Measuring the length of a curved line

cards.question: The length of a curved line can be measured by using a thread and a scale. We take a piece of thread and put a knot near its one end. This knot will act as a starting point for measuring the length of the curved line.
cards.text_html: The length of a curved line can be measured by using a thread and a scale. We take a piece of thread and put a knot near its one end. This knot will act as a starting point for measuring the length of the curved line.

cards.question: Motion : It is a state of objects in which they are moving, that is, they are changing their place with the changing time. For example - a table.
cards.text_html: Motion: It is a state of objects in which they are moving, that is, they are changing their place with the changing time. For example - a table.

cards.question: Rest: All the stationary objects which are not in motion, that is, do not change their place with time are said to be at rest. Example - the motion of an ant.
cards.text_html: Rest: All the stationary objects which are not in motion, that is, do not change their place with time are said to be at rest. Example - the motion of an ant.

cards.question: Rectilinear motion: This type of motion is a straight-line motion. Rectilinear motion takes place in a fixed direction.
cards.text_html: Rectilinear motion: This type of motion is a straight-line motion. Rectilinear motion takes place in a fixed direction.

cards.question: Examples of rectilinear motion: The movement of a bullet fired from a gun. The march-past of soldiers in a parade. The motion of a falling stone. The motion of a ball rolling on the ground. The motion of a cyclist running on a straight road.
cards.text_html:
Examples of rectilinear motion:

  1. The movement of a bullet fired from a gun.
  2. The march-past of soldiers in a parade.
  3. The motion of a falling stone.
  4. The motion of a ball rolling on the ground.
  5. The motion of a cyclist running on a straight road.

cards.question: Circular motion: A path having the shape of a circle is called a circular path. When an object moves along a circular path, it is called circular motion.
cards.text_html: Circular motion: A path having the shape of a circle is called a circular path. When an object moves along a circular path, it is called circular motion.

cards.question: Examples of circular motion: The movement of the moon around the earth. Movement of artificial satellites around the earth. Movement of the earth around the sun. The movement of the tip of the hand of the clock The movement of the blades of a fan.
cards.text_html:
Examples of circular motion:

  1. The movement of the moon around the earth.
  2. Movement of artificial satellites around the earth.
  3. Movement of the earth around the sun.
  4. The movement of the tip of the hand of the clock
  5. The movement of the blades of a fan.

cards.question: Periodic motion: Periodic motion is the motion that repeats itself after regular intervals of time.
cards.text_html: Periodic motion: Periodic motion is the motion that repeats itself after regular intervals of time.

cards.question: Examples of periodic motion: The motion of seconds’ hand of a watch. The revolution of the earth around the sun. The rotation of the earth on its axis. The revolution of the moon around the earth. Vibrations of the stretched membrane of the drum. The motion of the pendulum of a pendulum clock.
cards.text_html:
Examples of periodic motion:
  1. The motion of seconds’ hand of a watch.
  2. The revolution of the earth around the sun.
  3. The rotation of the earth on its axis.
  4. The revolution of the moon around the earth.
  5. Vibrations of the stretched membrane of the drum.
  6. The motion of the pendulum of a pendulum clock.

cards.question: Rotational motion: When an object turns or spins about a fixed axis, it is called rotational motion .
cards.text_html: Rotational motion: When an object turns or spins about a fixed axis, it is called rotational motion.

cards.question: Examples of rotational motion: The motion of a spinning top. The spinning of the earth on its axis. Turning off the blades of a fan. Turning of potter’s wheel. Turning of the bicycle wheel.
cards.text_html:
Examples of rotational motion:
  1. The motion of a spinning top.
  2. The spinning of the earth on its axis.
  3. Turning off the blades of a fan.
  4. Turning of potter’s wheel.
  5. Turning of the bicycle wheel.

cards.question: Combination of two or more types of motions: The motion of a ball on the ground. Here, the ball is rotating about an axis but the axis itself is moving along a straight line. Thus, the ball executes a rectilinear motion as well as a rotational motion. The motion of earth-earth executes rotations on its axis and also revolves around the sun.
cards.text_html:
Combination of two or more types of motions:

  • The motion of a ball on the ground. Here, the ball is rotating about an axis but the axis itself is moving along a straight line. Thus, the ball executes a rectilinear motion as well as a rotational motion.
  • The motion of earth-earth executes rotations on its axis and also revolves around the sun.
